1: Diamartyromai enōpion tou theou kai Christou Iēsou tou mellontos krinein zōntas kai nekrous (I solemnly charge before God and Christ Jesus who is about to judge the living and the dead): diamartyromai (I solemnly testify, I charge solemnly) — the intensive compound; the charge is given before the highest possible witnesses: God and Christ Jesus as the eschatological Judge. Zōntas kai nekrous (the living and the dead) — the universal scope of the judgment; all humanity regardless of their status at the Parousia (cf. 1 Peter 4:5; Acts 10:42). Kata tēn epiphaneian autou kai tēn basileian autou (in view of / by his appearing and his kingdom) — the appearing and the kingdom are the twin eschatological realities that give the charge its urgency.
2: Kērykson ton logon (herald the word): the bare imperative — no qualifications, no conditions; the herald proclaims regardless of circumstances. Episthī eukairōs akairōs (stand ready in season and out of season) — eukairōs (at the right time, in season) and akairōs (at the wrong time, out of season): the herald is ready when the audience is receptive and when they are not. Elenxon, epitimēson, parakaleson (reprove, rebuke, encourage) — three aorist imperatives covering the full ministerial range: reprove error (elenchō), rebuke personal sin (epitimaō), encourage the discouraged (parakaleō). En pasē makrothymia kai didachē (with all patience and teaching) — the manner that accompanies all three: patient and instructional rather than impulsive.
3: Estai gar kairos hote tēs hygiainousēs didaskalias ouk anexontai (for there will be a time when they will not endure sound teaching): the prediction echoes the last-days framework of 2 Timothy 3; the inability to endure sound teaching is itself one of the signs of the difficult times. Kata tas idias epithumias heautois episōreuousin didaskalous (according to their own desires they heap up for themselves teachers) — episōreuō (to heap up, accumulate) — the appetite-driven accumulation of teachers who confirm what the audience already wants to hear. Knēthomenoi tēn akoēn (having their hearing scratched/tickled) — the image of the ear-itch that wants to be scratched; the audience selects teachers who provide the satisfaction of agreeable stimulation rather than the discomfort of truth.
4: Apo men tēs alētheias tēn akoēn apostrepsousin, epi de tous mythous ektraperontai (they will turn their hearing from truth and will turn aside to myths): the double verb of turning — apostrephō (to turn away from) and ektrepō (to turn aside toward); the movement is away from truth and toward myths simultaneously. Mythous (myths) — the contrast between alētheia (truth) and mythos (myth/story) is a standard Platonic and Stoic distinction; the Pastoral Epistles use mythos for the false teachers' genealogies and speculations (1 Timothy 1:4; 2 Timothy 4:7; Titus 1:14).
5: Sy de nēphe en pasin, kakopathēson, ergon poiēson euangelistou, tēn diakonian sou plērophorēson (but you, be sober-minded in all things, endure hardship, do the work of an evangelist, fulfill your ministry): four aorist imperatives stacked against the drifting audience of 2 Timothy 4:3-4. Nēphō (to be sober, clear-headed) — the watchfulness that keeps Timothy alert when the surrounding culture is intoxicated with comfortable teaching. Plērophorēson tēn diakonian sou (fulfill/fully discharge your ministry) — plērophoreō (to fill completely, fully accomplish); the ministry is not partially discharged but brought to full completion.
6: Egō gar ēdē spendomai kai ho kairos tēs analyseōs mou ephestēken (for I am already being poured out as a drink offering and the time of my departure has come): spendomai (I am being poured out as a libation) — the present passive of spendō (to pour a libation at sacrifice); Paul's death is the libation poured at the conclusion of his sacrificial ministry (cf. Philippians 2:17: spendomai epi tē thysią, where it is an addition to others' sacrifice; here Paul is the sacrifice itself). Analysis (departure) — analuō means to loosen, untie, dissolve; it is the loosing of a ship's moorings to depart, or the striking of a tent for departure; an image of release rather than annihilation.
7: Ton kalon agōna ēgōnismai, ton dromon tetelekas, tēn pistin tetērēka (I have fought the good fight, I have finished the race, I have kept the faith): three perfect indicatives — ēgōnismai, tetelekas, tetērēka — describing completed action with present relevance. The triple perfect is the literary high point of the letter: a life summarized in three images. Kalos agōn (good fight/contest) — the same phrase as 1 Timothy 6:12; now Paul uses it of his own completed career. Dromos (race/course) — the athletic course run to completion. Pistin tetērēka (I have kept the faith) — faith as a deposit kept, echoing parathēkē (deposit to guard) from 2 Timothy 1:12-14.
8: Loipon apokeitai moi ho tēs dikaiosynēs stephanos (henceforth there is stored/laid up for me the crown of righteousness): apokeitai (is laid away, stored) — the crown is in eschatological storage, reserved for its recipient. Stephanos dikaiosynēs (crown of righteousness) — the victor's wreath made of righteousness; either the crown that righteousness produces or the crown that the righteous Judge awards. Ho dikaios kritēs (the righteous Judge) — Christ as the impartial eschatological judge whose verdict corresponds to reality. Pasin tois ēgapēkosi tēn epiphaneian autou (to all who have loved his appearing) — the crown is not exclusive to Paul but awarded to all who love the Parousia.
9: Spoudason elthein pros me tacheos (make every effort to come to me soon): spoudazō (to be diligent, make haste, earnestly endeavor) + tacheos (quickly, soon) — the urgency of the summons. The personal request for Timothy's presence after the solemn peroration of 2 Timothy 4:6-8 grounds the theology in the practical humanity of the imprisoned apostle who wants his beloved companion near. The letter oscillates between the universal (the eschatological judge, the crown for all believers) and the particular (come before winter, bring the cloak).
10: Dēmas gar me enkatelipe agapēsas ton nyn aiōna (for Demas has deserted me, having loved the present age): agapēsas ton nyn aiōna (having loved the present age) — the aorist participle gives the theological reason for desertion. Ton nyn aiōna (the present age) — the current world order in its temporal, passing character; Demas chose love of the present age over solidarity with Paul who was suffering for the coming age. Demas appears in Philemon 24 and Colossians 4:14 as a fellow worker; his desertion is the more poignant for his prior close association with Paul.
11: Loukas estin monos met' emou (Luke alone is with me): the stark isolation — only Luke, the physician and author of Luke-Acts (cf. Colossians 4:14), remains. Markon analabōn age meta seautou (take Mark and bring him with you) — Mark (John Mark) had been a point of contention between Paul and Barnabas (Acts 15:37-39); his rehabilitation is marked here by Paul's warm commendation: estin gar moi euchrēstos eis diakonian (he is useful to me for ministry). The same adjective euchrēstos (useful) appears in 2:21 for the honored vessel; Mark has become the useful, honored vessel.
12: Tychikon de apesteila eis Epheson (Tychicus I sent to Ephesus): Tychicus appears as Paul's messenger in Ephesians 6:21-22, Colossians 4:7-8, Titus 3:12, and Acts 20:4 — a trusted emissary dispatched for specific missions. The sending of Tychicus to Ephesus (Timothy's location) may explain Timothy's being freed to come to Rome: his replacement has been dispatched. The letter's practical network of colleagues and messengers reveals the organizational complexity of Paul's mission.
13: Ton phelonēn hon apelipon en Trōadi para Karpō (the cloak I left at Troas with Carpus): phelonēs (cloak, travelling coat) — a heavy outer garment for warmth and weather protection; essential for a Roman winter. The request has pastoral import: Paul is cold in prison and winter is coming (2 Timothy 4:21). Kai ta biblia, malista tas membranas (and the books, especially the parchments) — biblia may be papyrus scrolls of Scripture or documents; membranas (parchments, membranae) is a Latin loanword, referring to the animal-skin writing surfaces; the parchments may be Paul's personal copies of Scripture or important documents. The apostle in prison wants his books.
14: Alexandros ho chalkeus polla moi kaka enedeixato (Alexander the metalworker/coppersmith did me a great deal of harm): chalkeus = worker in copper/bronze; possibly the Alexander of 1 Timothy 1:20 (though a common name). Apodōsei autō ho Kyrios kata ta erga autou (the Lord will repay him according to his works) — the passive future apodōsei (will repay) leaves vengeance to divine justice rather than claiming personal retribution; Paul cites the principle of Proverbs 24:12 / Psalms 62:12 (God repays according to deeds). The tone is judicial rather than vindictive.
15: Hon kai sy phylassou (whom you also guard against): the singular imperative of watchfulness. Lian gar antestē tois hēmeterois logois (for he strongly opposed our words/message) — lian (greatly, exceedingly) intensifies the opposition; antestē (he withstood, opposed) is the same verb used in 2 Timothy 4:8 for Jannes and Jambres opposing Moses. The pattern of opposition to apostolic teaching has a type (the Egyptian magicians), a local instance (Alexander), and a universal principle (the last-days difficult times).
16: En tē prōtē mou apologia oudeis moi paregento (at my first defense no one supported me): apologia (defense, formal legal defense) — the Roman judicial proceeding where the defendant makes their case; prōtē (first) implies there was a subsequent hearing. Pantes me enkatelipon (everyone deserted me) — the same verb as 2 Timothy 4:10 (Demas deserted); Paul was abandoned at his most critical moment. Mē autois logistheiē (may it not be held against them) — the optative of forgiveness echoing Stephen's prayer (Acts 7:60) and Jesus's cross-word (Luke 23:34).
17: Ho de Kyrios moi parestē kai enedynamōsen me (but the Lord stood alongside me and strengthened me): the contrast with 2 Timothy 4:16 — humans deserted, but the Lord stood beside (parestē) Paul; the same word used for a legal advocate standing alongside the defendant. Hina di' emou to kērygma plērophorēthē (so that through me the proclamation might be fully made) — even at his first defense, Paul's purpose is proclamation: the trial becomes a preaching opportunity before the Gentile powers (panta ta ethnē akousē). Errysthēn ek stomatos leontos (I was rescued from the lion's mouth) — Psalms 22:21 language; the lion may be a metaphor for the Roman court, Nero, or the devil (cf. 1 Peter 5:8).
18: Ryssetai me ho Kyrios apo pantos ergou ponērou kai sōsei eis tēn basileian autou tēn epouranion (the Lord will rescue me from every evil attack and will save me into his heavenly kingdom): the future tense: not rescue from physical death (Paul expects death, 2 Timothy 4:6-7) but rescue through death into the kingdom. Ergou ponērou (evil work/attack) — the evil that would destroy Paul's ultimate standing, not merely his physical life. Tēn basileian tēn epouranion (the heavenly kingdom) — the kingdom Paul will enter through death; the kingdom is already present in Christ but its final form is the eschatological destination. Hō hē doxa eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn — the doxological response triggered by confidence in divine rescue.
19: Aspasai Priskan kai Akylān (greet Prisca and Aquila): the notable pair mentioned in Romans 16:3, 1 Corinthians 16:19, and Acts 18:2, 18, 26; Priscilla (here "Prisca") is named first, as often — an indication of her prominence in the ministry partnership. They are apparently back in Ephesus (they had been in Corinth and Rome at various points). Kai ton Onēsiphorou oikon (and the household of Onesiphorus) — the same person praised in 2 Timothy 1:16-18; the greeting to his household (rather than to Onesiphorus himself) may imply his death.
20: Erastos emeinen en Korinthō, Trophimon de apelipon en Milētō asthenounta (Erastus remained in Corinth, and Trophimus I left sick at Miletus): the practical logistics of Paul's last journey. Erastus appears in Acts 19:22 and Romans 16:23 (where he is the city treasurer of Corinth — a significant civic position). Trophimus appears in Acts 20:4 and 2 Timothy 21:29 (an Ephesian, whose presence in the temple precincts triggered Paul's arrest). The detail that Paul left Trophimus sick (asthenounta) is significant: despite the Spirit's gifts of healing in the church, Paul did not simply heal his colleague — illness remains a reality within the community of faith.
21: Spoudason pro cheimōnos elthein (make every effort to come before winter): the practical urgency — winter made Mediterranean sea travel impossible (roughly from October to March; Acts 27:9-12 illustrates the danger). If Timothy did not arrive before winter, he could not come until spring, by which time Paul's execution might have occurred. Aspazontai se Eubulos kai Poudens kai Linos kai Klaudia kai hoi adelphoi pantes — the named Roman Christians: Pudens and Claudia appear in later tradition as early British converts (Tertullian); Linus appears in Irenaeus's list as the first bishop of Rome after Peter and Paul. These details, if authentic, connect the letter directly to the Roman church around the time of Paul's martyrdom.
22: Ho Kyrios meta tou pneumatos sou (the Lord be with your spirit): the singular sou (your, singular) addresses Timothy specifically — a personal benediction for the individual minister. Hē charis meth' hymōn (grace be with you all) — the plural hymōn signals the public reading dimension: behind the individual addressee is the congregation. The double closing — one singular, one plural — captures the letter's dual address throughout: Timothy the individual leader and the Ephesian community he serves. The brevity of the closing (hē charis alone, without doxology) matches the letter's tone of urgent intimacy.
